creator: ant_mail@inbox.ru
text:
Dear Mr. McKusick,
thank you for explanation.

But I've come to gjournal leaded by the experience.

I've used classic UFS+SU with background_fsck="YES" in the past. But ended with production server rebooted after every 5-10 minutes. This begun immediatly after rebooting after some software crash (system hangs). The reason I've found in logs. Message was something like:

FSCK: CAN'T WRITE
PANIC

As I understand background fsck was doing it job but when it reach part of filesystem  with inconsistency it discover itself in read-only mode and panic to not leave filesystem with inconsistency.

So I'm beware of using background fsck.

Even without backcground fsck I had problems with too many errors in filesystem  after crash if I remember correctly.

By this way I've come to gjournal.

I've also experienced problems with GEOM, here one of:

https://bugs.freebsd.org/bugzilla/show_bug.cgi?id=274103

It's very frustrating when you reboot production server and it hangs. You insert live USB stick and it hangs the same in geom_mirror.ko. So I'm not going to ZFS fully because of scare that sometime somewhere system will hang in zfs.ko

As of recent addition to UFS that allow fully functional SU+J... I've read somewhere that it will leave zero length file after recovery by design.

That is the question. Which files will become zero-sized? The best answer is: files which was in the middle of writing when crash happen. But I don't know the truth.

What about files just opened for RW? What about files fwrite()'d 5 minutes ago? 10 minutes? 30 minutes?

There are very few, if any, files doing write at the moment of crash. But a lot of opened for RW  and writed some time ago.

It's very hard and riscy way to explore this by upgrading servers and recompile all packages. Also my experience say to me to wait till FreeBSD 14.2 or 14.4 arrive.

Back to snapshots... I have another partitions with gjournal+UFS. They never hangs with snapshoting.

May you suggest something to tune to minimize the probability of hang during snapshot removing? May be there is a snapshot size limit exceeding which will definitely become a problem?
